findings.title: Dangerous import: IoDeleteSymbolicLink
findings.description: Deletes symbolic link — racy deletion enables stale-link hijack

findings.title: Dangerous import: IoCreateDevice
findings.description: Creates device object — check if SecurityDescriptor is NULL

findings.title: Dangerous import: MmMapIoSpace
findings.description: Maps physical memory — arbitrary R/W primitive if address is user-controlled

findings.title: Dangerous import: IoCreateSymbolicLink
findings.description: Creates symbolic link — check accessibility

findings.title: No ProbeForRead/ProbeForWrite imports found
findings.description: Driver imports dangerous APIs but never imports ProbeForRead or ProbeForWrite. User buffer validation is likely missing.

findings.title: Section 'INIT' is Writable + Executable (W+X)
findings.description: Section has both write and execute permissions. This is a strong indicator of self-modifying code or packing.

findings.title: Double-fetch (TOCTOU) at 0x112AD
findings.description: Two reads from [reg+0x18] at 0x1122B and 0x112AD with a cmp/test using the first value and the second value flowing into a sensitive sink (allocator / memcpy / deref). An attacker can race to change the buffer between check and use.

findings.title: Missing mitigation: DEP/NX (/NXCOMPAT)
findings.description: DEP/NX (/NXCOMPAT) is not enabled. Executable pages usable for shellcode without ROP chains.

findings.title: Missing mitigation: ASLR (/DYNAMICBASE)
findings.description: ASLR (/DYNAMICBASE) is not enabled. Driver loads at predictable address — KASLR bypass trivial.

findings.title: Missing mitigation: Signature enforcement (FORCE_INTEGRITY)
findings.description: Signature enforcement (FORCE_INTEGRITY) is not enabled. Driver can load without a valid Authenticode signature.

findings.title: Missing mitigation: Control Flow Guard (/guard:cf)
findings.description: Control Flow Guard (/guard:cf) is not enabled. Indirect calls unvalidated — control-flow hijacking easier.

findings.title: Missing mitigation: Stack canary (/GS)
findings.description: Stack canary (/GS) is not enabled. Stack overflows not detected by compiler canary check.

findings.title: Missing mitigation: High-entropy ASLR (/HIGHENTROPYVA)
findings.description: High-entropy ASLR (/HIGHENTROPYVA) is not enabled. 64-bit ASLR entropy reduced — address brute-force more feasible.

findings.title: Driver is NOT HVCI-compatible
findings.description: Driver fails HVCI checks (missing FORCE_INTEGRITY or has W+X sections). Cannot load in HVCI-enabled environments without policy bypass.

findings.title: Kernel write gadgets found (8 instances)
findings.description: Multiple 'mov [reg+offset], reg' patterns in driver code. If the register is derived from user input (e.g., IOCTL buffer), this is an arbitrary kernel memory write primitive.

details.gadgets: 0x11036: mov qword ptr [rsp + 0x30], r11; 0x1107B: mov qword ptr [rbx + 0x70], rax; 0x1107F: mov qword ptr [rbx + 0x80], rax; 0x11086: mov qword ptr [rbx + 0xe0], rax

findings.title: Hidden function with purpose 'phys mem map' at 0x110E8
findings.description: Function at 0x110E8 performs 'phys mem map' but is not reachable via the normal DriverEntry → MajorFunction call graph.  It may be invoked via a timer, DPC, work item, or kernel callback — a hidden secondary attack path.

findings.title: Hidden function with purpose 'phys mem map' at 0x11519
findings.description: Function at 0x11519 performs 'phys mem map' but is not reachable via the normal DriverEntry → MajorFunction call graph.  It may be invoked via a timer, DPC, work item, or kernel callback — a hidden secondary attack path.

findings.title: No PreviousMode / RequestorMode check detected
findings.description: Driver does not import ExGetPreviousMode or KeGetPreviousMode and no inline KTHREAD.PreviousMode read was found. Without checking PreviousMode, a kernel-mode caller could be impersonated, or user-mode requests may bypass validation. IOCTLs should verify RequestorMode == UserMode before trusting input.

findings.title: IOCTL handlers missing InputBufferLength validation
findings.description: The following IOCTL handlers do not appear to validate InputBufferLength before processing the buffer: 0x9C406104. Without size checks, a short buffer could cause pool overread or out-of-bounds writes.

findings.title: IOCTL handlers missing NULL buffer check
findings.description: These IOCTL handlers may not check for NULL SystemBuffer before dereferencing: 0x9C402000, 0x9C402004, 0x9C402084, 0x9C402088, 0x9C40208C, 0x9C402090, 0x9C4060C4, 0x9C406104, 0x9C406144, 0x9C40A0C8, 0x9C40A108, 0x9C40A148. A zero-length IOCTL request can result in SystemBuffer=NULL, causing a kernel NULL-pointer dereference (BSOD).

findings.title: No privilege check APIs imported
findings.description: Driver handles IOCTLs but does not import SeSinglePrivilegeCheck, SePrivilegeCheck, or SeAccessCheck. Sensitive operations should verify caller privileges beyond just device handle access rights.

findings.title: IoCreateDevice without FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN
findings.description: Driver uses IoCreateDevice instead of IoCreateDeviceSecure and FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN flag was not detected. Without this flag, the device object inherits permissive default security, allowing any user to open a handle. Use IoCreateDeviceSecure with an SDDL string to restrict access.

findings.title: No SDDL security descriptor found
findings.description: No SDDL security descriptor string detected in the binary. Drivers should apply SDDL-based ACLs to their device objects to control which users/groups can open handles.

findings.title: Driver has Authenticode signature
findings.description: PE contains a WIN_CERTIFICATE structure with embedded signature.

findings.title: Signed by: GlobalSign Time Stamping Authority
findings.description:
Subject: GlobalSign Time Stamping Authority (GlobalSign)
Issuer: GlobalSign RootSign Partners CA
Serial: 400000000011092eb8295
Key: RSA 2048-bit
Valid until: 2014-01-27T09:00:00+00:00

findings.title: Signing certificate EXPIRED (valid until 2014-01-27T09:00:00+00:00)
findings.description: The code-signing certificate has expired. While drivers with a valid timestamp counter-signature remain valid after expiry, an expired cert without a timestamp means the signature is no longer trusted. This is also common in BYOVD — old vulnerable drivers with expired certs that were signed before revocation.

findings.title: Signer certificate lacks Code Signing EKU
findings.description: The signing certificate has Extended Key Usage extensions but does NOT include codeSigning (1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.3). This certificate was not intended for code signing. Some older drivers were signed with mismatched EKU certs.

findings.title: Expired certificates in chain: Noriyuki MIYAZAKI, GlobalSign RootSign Partners CA, GlobalSign Primary Object Publishing CA, GlobalSign ObjectSign CA, GlobalSign Root CA
findings.description: One or more certificates in the signing chain have expired. If the root or intermediate CA cert has expired, the entire chain of trust is broken unless a valid timestamp exists.

findings.title: Signing certificate uses deprecated SHA-1 algorithm
findings.description: The signing certificate uses SHA-1 hash algorithm which has been deprecated by Microsoft since 2020. SHA-1 signed drivers may be rejected on Windows 10 1903+ and will not pass WHQL attestation signing. SHA-256 or stronger required.

findings.title: PKCS#7 signature verifies cryptographically
findings.description: The primary Authenticode PKCS#7 signature was verified against the signer certificate's public key.

findings.title: Authenticode PE hash matches (binary not tampered)
findings.description: Recomputed sha1 hash of the PE image matches the value embedded in SpcIndirectDataContent.

findings.title: Only SHA-1 Authenticode signature — rejected on modern Windows
findings.description: Primary signature uses SHA-1 and no SHA-256 nested signature is present. Windows 10 1903+ / HVCI systems will reject this driver at load time.

findings.title: Chain anchored to kernel-trusted root (ms-root-referenced)
findings.description: Chain terminates at 'Microsoft Code Verification Root' — recognised as kernel-trusted (ms-root-referenced).

findings.title: HVCI incompatible: missing FORCE_INTEGRITY flag, has W+X sections
findings.description: This driver is NOT compatible with Hypervisor-protected Code Integrity (HVCI / Memory Integrity). On systems with HVCI enabled, Windows will BLOCK this driver from loading. Issues: missing FORCE_INTEGRITY flag, has W+X sections.

findings.title: Driver architecture: x64
findings.description: 64-bit driver — loads on x64 Windows

findings.title: Load compatibility: BLOCKED — 1 issue(s) prevent loading
findings.description:
[PASS] Expired + Timestamped: Expired cert but has valid timestamp — signature accepted
  [BLOCKED] HVCI: Blocked when Memory Integrity is ON (missing FORCE_INTEGRITY flag, has W+X sections)
  [PASS] Secure Boot: Compatible (signed driver)
  [PASS] Timestamp: Expired cert but timestamped by Noriyuki MIYAZAKI

passes: Signed 2008-07-26 (pre-expiry) — cert expiry ignored by Windows
Signed 2008-07-26 (pre-expiry) — cert expiry ignored by Windows

blockers: FORCE_INTEGRITY flag not set (HVCI requires it on the PE header)
passes: Signed 2008-07-26 (pre-expiry) — cert expiry ignored by Windows
Any valid signature accepted under test-signing

blockers: No WHQL attestation EKU — S Mode / Secured-Core require WHQL-signed drivers
No Authenticode page hashes (Secured-Core requires per-page integrity)

findings.title: Device Access Security Audit
findings.description: Uses IoCreateDevice (not IoCreateDeviceSecure); Missing FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN flag; Device is not exclusive (multiple handles allowed); Exposed via symlinks: \DosDevices\WinRing0_1_2_0; No SDDL security descriptor — default permissive ACL

findings.title: Taint reaches MmMapIoSpace via arg4(r9)
findings.description: User-controlled IOCTL input (code 0x9C406104) reaches dangerous sink MmMapIoSpace through 1 call depth(s). Tainted arguments: arg4(r9).

findings.title: ROP/JOP gadgets found: 73 total (reg-control: 32, memory-read: 26, misc: 13, stack-pivot: 2)
findings.description:
Found 73 usable ROP/JOP gadgets in executable sections. Since many drivers lack ASLR, these gadgets are at predictable addresses and can be chained for kernel exploitation.

Top gadgets by type:
  [stack-pivot] (2 found):
    0x11415: pop rsp ; and al, 0x40 ; add rsp, 0x30 ; pop rdi ; ret
    0x115DF: pop rsp ; ret
  [memory-read] (26 found):
    0x110BA: mov ecx, dword ptr [rsp + 0x80] ; call qword ptr [rip + 0xf61] ; mov eax, ebx ; add rsp, 0x60 ; pop rbx ; ret
    0x11414: mov ebx, dword ptr [rsp + 0x40] ; add rsp, 0x30 ; pop rdi ; ret
    0x11413: mov rbx, qword ptr [rsp + 0x40] ; add rsp, 0x30 ; pop rdi ; ret
  [reg-control] (32 found):
    0x110CD: pop rbx ; ret
    0x110CA: add esp, 0x60 ; pop rbx ; ret
    0x110C9: add rsp, 0x60 ; pop rbx ; ret

findings.title: State machine: 132 cross-IOCTL dependencies found
findings.description:
Analysis of global variable access patterns reveals 132 state dependencies between IOCTL handlers. One IOCTL writes a global that another IOCTL reads, creating an implicit state machine.

Dependencies:
  0x9C402000 → 0x9C402004 (1 shared global(s) at 0x13110)
  0x9C402000 → 0x9C402084 (1 shared global(s) at 0x13110)
  0x9C402000 → 0x9C402088 (1 shared global(s) at 0x13110)
  0x9C402000 → 0x9C40208C (1 shared global(s) at 0x13110)
  0x9C402000 → 0x9C402090 (1 shared global(s) at 0x13110)
  0x9C402000 → 0x9C4060C4 (1 shared global(s) at 0x13110)
  0x9C402000 → 0x9C406104 (1 shared global(s) at 0x13110)
  0x9C402000 → 0x9C406144 (1 shared global(s) at 0x13110)
  0x9C402000 → 0x9C40A0C8 (1 shared global(s) at 0x13110)
  0x9C402000 → 0x9C40A108 (1 shared global(s) at 0x13110)
